summary: Joe Reis argues that the data industry's relentless buzzword churn—now pivoting from 'Year of Agents' to 'Year of Context'—is actively harmful because it piles new trends onto unfinished foundational work. Companies that can't get basic BI and dashboards working are being pressured to implement AI agents and context pipelines built on top of poorly modeled data. The real beneficiaries are vendors whose business models depend on keeping the hype flywheel spinning.
key_points: New buzzwords are being stacked on broken foundations before organizations have implemented previous ones
Most companies still struggle with basic BI and data quality, making AI agent adoption premature
'Context' as a concept introduces data governance challenges at least as hard as traditional data modeling, but with less tooling and fewer practitioners
The hype cycle serves vendors more than practitioners, pressuring data leaders to chase trends at the expense of execution
Core challenges like people and processes have remained unchanged for decades despite successive hype cycles
money_quotes: The dark irony here? A lot of these teams can barely get their f*cking dashboards working. But AI will do away with dashboards, right?
The Buzzword Industrial Complex will try to convince you otherwise, mostly to keep its flywheel of vendor rankings and hype cycles spinning.
Buying a shiny new toy doesn't atone for past architectural sins.
We're stacking new trends on broken foundations. We haven't even finished the last several 'seasons' of buzzwords, and now we're onto a new one.
If each hype cycle magically solved everything, we wouldn't still be having the exact same conversations about core challenges, like people and processes, that we've been having for decades.
key_insight: The industry's compulsive buzzword cycling is vendor-driven performance theater that harms organizations by pushing them toward AI and 'context' initiatives before they've solved foundational data quality and modeling problems.
